content:
This is an idea for a single new syntax ..x that could be used in various different places of the language to simplify things.

List patterns

This change is the smallest one of them all, and is basically just to allow patterns like [first, ..middle, last] as a short form for [first, .. as middle, last] in when patterns. This is just to stay consistent with list interpolation.

List interpolation

Roc currently has string interpolation, which makes working with strings really nice, but when you're working with lists, you have to piece them together by using List.append, List.concat, etc., which is harder to read and write than interpolation, and which is also possibly less efficient because the final length of the list isn't known when the first part of it is allocated. My proposal is to use the same [first, ..middle, last] syntax also for list interpolation.

With these changes, a simple unoptimized textbook quicksort could look like this:

quickSort = \list ->
    when list is
        [] -> []
        [..rest, pivot] ->
            lessEq = List.keepIf rest (\x -> x <= pivot)
            greater = List.keepIf rest (\x -> x > pivot)

            [..quickSort lessEq, pivot, ..quickSort greater]

The list literal in the end is very readable, and the length of the list is easy for the compiler to know before creating it.

Tag union types

The notation for tag union types in Roc is essentially the same as the notation for lists, so a tag union type is just a list of tags. That's very natural and easy to understand. What isn't necessarily easy to understand is the syntax for open tag unions and for union types.

I would suggest just using the same list interpolation syntax also for types:

WarmColor : [Red, Orange, Yellow]
ColdColor : [Green, Blue, Violet]
Color : [Black, White, ..WarmColor, ..ColdColor]

I consider this to be more readable than the current syntax

Color : [Black, White] WarmColor ColdColor

This would also make open tag unions much easier to understand. Instead of the cryptic syntax [Red, Yellow, Green]a, you could just write [Red, Yellow, Green, ..a] which has an obvious meaning to anybody who knows how to work with lists, and instead of [Red, Yellow, Green]*, you could just write [Red, Yellow, Green, ..] which is much more self-explanatory.

Record types

Obviously, the same syntax could also be used for records, so instead of { first : Str, last : Str }* you'd write { first : Str, last : Str, .. } and instead of { first : Str, last : Str }a you'd write { first : Str, last : Str, ..a }.

Record patterns and literals (optional)

An optional extension to this would be to use the same syntax also in record patterns and literals, which means you could use records very flexibly, including adding and deleting fields.

removeLastName : { first : Str, last : Str, ..a } -> { first : Str, ..a }
removeLastName = \{ first, last : _, ..rest } -> { first, ..rest }

That's a lot of uses for one tool, but definitely seems like it might be reasonable. I think using .. with multiple following values is not very readable (e.g. ..quickSort lessEq), but otherwise each of these alone feels like an ok syntax. Not sure we want so many overloads for the same .. though.

Fritz Psiorz said:

An optional extension to this would be to use the same syntax also in record patterns and literals, which means you could use records very flexibly, including adding and deleting fields.

removeLastName : { first : Str, last : Str, ..a } -> { first : Str, ..a }
removeLastName = \{ first, last : _, ..rest } -> { first, ..rest }

interesting! I think we could extend the current type system for records to support this without impacting compiler performance, but @Ayaz Hafiz or @Folkert de Vries would have a better intuition for that than I would :big_smile:

@Brendan Hansknecht I actually decided that ..x is a better syntax than x.. for this reason: you see up front that it's a list interpolation rather than a single item in a list, even if it's a more complex expression.

Whether it's good style to use such a long expression in a list literal is a different question, but that's not specific to interpolations.
I think this is specific to the .. interpolation. It is an order of operations question.
The difference between this being correct or a bug ..quickSort lessEq. For example, we could require `..(quickSort lessEq) or always using a variable with .. never an expression.

This would also make open tag unions much easier to understand. Instead of the cryptic syntax [Red, Yellow, Green]a, you could just write [Red, Yellow, Green, ..a] which has an obvious meaning to anybody who knows how to work with lists, and instead of [Red, Yellow, Green]*, you could just write [Red, Yellow, Green, ..] which is much more self-explanatory.

I like this a lot, although we may have talked about something like it before

Brendan Hansknecht schrieb:

The difference between this being correct or a bug ..quickSort lessEq. For example, we could require `..(quickSort lessEq) or always using a variable with .. never an expression.

Possibly just adding a space in the formatter might do the trick. .. quickSort lessEq reads more like .. is applied to everything that follows rather than just to quickSort.
I disagree. Following roc expected syntax. That looks more like .. is taking quickSort and lessEq both as arguments. Which would be an invalid use of .. which only takes one argument.

I'm not really passionate about the issue, but I'd see .. as a part of the syntax for writing lists, like [, ], and ,, and not as a part of the syntax of the actual list elements. We also allow arbitrary expressions between commas in list literals and we don't require parentheses around those expressions. But requiring parentheses in the beginning also wouldn't hurt, and removing that requirement later wouldn't break anybody's program.

could use parens:

quickSort = \list ->
    when list is
        [] -> []
        [..rest, pivot] ->
            lessEq = List.keepIf rest (\x -> x <= pivot)
            greater = List.keepIf rest (\x -> x > pivot)

            [..(quickSort lessEq), pivot, ..(quickSort greater)]

Fritz Psiorz said:

... [Red, Yellow, Green]*, you could just write [Red, Yellow, Green, ..] which is much more self-explanatory.

This would be so nice. I think most people would get it immediately.
I agree, and it feels less confusing than *
We should definitely double check the edge cases with * and see if they seem less confusing with this
I guess you still need * for variables in alias apply expressions, such as Num *
Num .. ? :smile:
Oh, also, this would replace record update syntax?
I know we avoid that original cause it would lead to tons of extra data movement if you add or remove a field
That or it would have banned cases that would confuse new users
I don't think the current type system supports that, though
(adding/removing fields)
I don't think it would be hard to support. It is just almost always the wrong answer
A generic function like removeLastName probably should never exist

In Elm, sometimes you need to call a function that takes almost the same record that you already have plus a few more fields. In those cases, you have to manually copy all fields instead of updating, which is cumbersome.
I know Elm used to be able to add/remove fields and that got removed. I wasn't around though, so I don't know what were the main motivations.
In roc, removing is almost never needed. You can essentially pass a view in by using an open record
Adding is a problem

Agus Zubiaga said:

I know Elm used to be able to add/remove fields and that got removed. I wasn't around though, so I don't know what were the main motivations.

there was a runtime performance downside to supporting it, although I don't remember exactly what it was anymore. I think it might have had something to do with the generated code always dynamically add fields to JS objects, which messed up the JIT in comparison to always knowing you for sure had the exact same set of fields

So roc definitely could.monomorphize over all of this, but I think it is a case of promoting a pattern that is bad for performance. Adding fields to a record can mean a ton of data movement. It is better to nest records, pass extra args, or similar.
I wouldn't honestly expect it to come up that often
seems like the type of thing where you want to reach for it infrequently, but when you do want to reach for it, it's very nice to be able to access it
Users already try to use record update syntax as a dictionary. So I would expect it to come up frequently.

Brendan Hansknecht schrieb:

Oh, also, this would replace record update syntax?

In obviously wouldn't, but you could indeed technically replace it by a function of this type:

update : { ..a, ..b }, b -> { ..a, ..b }

Then you could just use something like this

{ a: 1, b: "hello", c: "world"} |> Record.update { b: "goodbye" }

I'm not seriously suggesting this.

Oh, I just realized that it has different semantics. This wouldn't work with the .. syntax as proposed:

state = {a: 1, b: 2, c: 3}
{ a: 7, ..state}
@Brendan Hansknecht Why not?
It just isn't part of the original proposal.
It could be done, but that is a different use case from the proposal above
Ah, sure. I think we were just discussing it because it would make sense to make it all uniform.
This is filling the role a in { first : Str, last : Str }a. It is invalid for a to contain first or last cause those are already part of the original record.

I think we were just discussing it because it would make sense to make it all uniform.

For sure. That said, I think I may actually prefer it if .. is just for additive combinations and never for updates and such.

I didn't include it because I didn't want to make the proposal even bigger, but also because I don't think it's a good idea. Update syntax guarantees that the type stays the same. No fields are added, or removed, or have their type changed. And you don't want to do those things on accident.
Yeah, I guess the record update and open record syntax doesn't match already, so the status quo would be preserved without it

I do think an infix syntax for an update would be better (and I think there's a proposal for that somewhere), and I could see some potential of using the new syntax in there for updating more than one field at once.
For example you have something like name = { firstName: "John", lastName: "Doe" } somewhere and then you do something like person & { ..name, age: 35 } to update three fields at once.

I mean is that really any different than allowing .. in the current syntax:
{ person & ..name, age: 35 }
I don't think the infix syntax is needed.

It's not really different, no. The advantage of the infix syntax is that it's potentially just an operator, and there are just basic records on both sides. So even just person & name could work for updating a two fields. This seems like it would reduce potential edge cases. It's also better for pipelining. But that's really unrelated to this proposal. The current syntax would work with it just fine.

Richard Feldman said:

Agus Zubiaga said:

I know Elm used to be able to add/remove fields and that got removed. I wasn't around though, so I don't know what were the main motivations.

there was a runtime performance downside to supporting it, although I don't remember exactly what it was anymore. I think it might have had something to do with the generated code always dynamically add fields to JS objects, which messed up the JIT in comparison to always knowing you for sure had the exact same set of fields

update: I asked Evan and he doesn't remember anymore either :laughing:
Found the post for when it was removed: https://elm-lang.org/news/compilers-as-assistants#simplified-records
It looks like it was about discouraging overly complex code and improving JS perf
More context: https://github.com/elm/compiler/issues/985

One other discrepancy of this syntax:

toMatch = [1, 2, 3]
when someList is
    [..toMatch, pivot] ->
        # Without the `as` it feels like the above should be valid.
        # Basically this would match a list [1, 2, 3, pivot] where it captures pivot as a variable.

Obviously it wouldn't work, but I think sharing the syntax makes it feel like it should work.
That said, this also doesn't work, so maybe it isn't a problem:

pivot = 7
when someList is
    [.., pivot] ->
        # the goal was matching a list ending in `pivot` aka 7
I think that should cause a shadowing error, so it might be fine
Unless we allow shadowing, but I wonder if even then we should allow this case
I assume if a user wrote the above code, they would expect to match on the dynamic value of toMatch or pivot. So probably want a special case error for it.
This is not specific to .., you can run into this today
We probably need some sigil or something if we want to support this. Without it, this is introducing a new symbol, not referencing one.
Yeah, realized that with the pivot example.

Anyway, I would say that I am roughly in favor of must of these ideas. That said, not sure about record field addition and removal in a generic form. I think it is likely to generate really terrible assembly and we shouldn't promote it.

I think it would probably be fine if we allow using ..record with update syntax though. So It can't add new fields or remove existing fields, but it can update fields:

person = { first: "", last: "", age: 0, ..., field27: [] }
name = { first: "John", last: "Doe" }
{ person & ..name, age: 35 }

This idea seems pretty good, I'm just not sure how it should work if we pass a superset of the fields in the base record. Some options:

  1. Compiler error: you can only use the spread update for common fields
  2. Warning: we only use the fields in common, but remind you that we're only using the common fields
  3. No warning: just update the common fields, hope our tutorial prevents confusion
2 cause compiler errors are just warning. At least that is the hope.

But otherwise I agree with Brendan, I like most of this syntax! I like anything that provides a way to use a smaller set of syntax tools to express everything in Roc, especially the tag union alias spreading.
I guess the equivalent of 1 would be compile warning plus runtime error when running the record update.
Well, is it not possible for us to identify that the spread record has fields not present in the base record and make that a type error? I think I'm missing something

A goal of roc is to always be able to run even with compile errors. So even a type error should compile (emitting a warning), but only full fail with a runtime error if the branch with bad types is hit.
Oh, you mean for dev mode vs. release, right?
Yes, a runtime error for dev mode makes sense
Yeah

Also, just to clarify my statement above a bit more. I see two valid options around errors if you pass a record to an update and it has extra fields:

  1. Emit a compiler error that is like a type checking error. Run the program anyway, but throw a runtime error if the invalid code is executed. (Runtime error would probably be that it can't add extra fields to the record using record update)
  2. Emit a compiler warning. Run the program anyway. No runtime error at all. Just update the relevant fields and ignore the extras.

Personally, I would learn towards 2, but both are fine

I think it should be an error because ..x would generally mean "spread all or x here.

Basically: { record & ..{ a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 } } should always be identical to { record & a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }. So the behavior of spreading a record with extra fields in an update should be the same as you already get when trying to update fields that don't exist.

Richard Feldman said:

Richard Feldman said:

Agus Zubiaga said:

I know Elm used to be able to add/remove fields and that got removed. I wasn't around though, so I don't know what were the main motivations.

there was a runtime performance downside to supporting it, although I don't remember exactly what it was anymore. I think it might have had something to do with the generated code always dynamically add fields to JS objects, which messed up the JIT in comparison to always knowing you for sure had the exact same set of fields

update: I asked Evan and he doesn't remember anymore either :laughing:

Evan pinged me with a relevant detail he remembered: the original paper allowed duplicate fields (e.g. { foo: 1, foo: 2 } would be a valid record) and updates had to do extra work to update the correct one (if I remember right, that's because the semantics of the paper were to update the last field with that name, not the first one)
Evan noted that he thinks monomorphization could resolve that problem
(because if you end up with multiple fields after type checking, you can monomorphize to remove the duplicates)
I think it would be a good idea for someone to put together a sort of "maximum .." doc
in other words, "here are all the different uses of .. we can think of that seam plausible, including replacing existing syntax like & in record updates"
and then we can take a step back and be like "ok this one doesn't seem worth it, but this other one does" etc.

Oh funny enough I was preparing the same proposal @Fritz Psiorz

Roc Proposal: Spread syntax for Open/Constrained Record and Open/Constrained Tag Unions
Maybe @Richard Feldman that's what you are looking for ?
nice, yeah! Are there any others to add to that list from this thread?

Fritz Psiorz said:

It's not really different, no. The advantage of the infix syntax is that it's potentially just an operator, and there are just basic records on both sides. So even just person & name could work for updating a two fields. This seems like it would reduce potential edge cases. It's also better for pipelining. But that's really unrelated to this proposal. The current syntax would work with it just fine.

I agree with @Fritz Psiorz that & operator having a record on the left and fields on the right tripped me at first.

Additionally the precedence rule between the & operator and the , is odd.

For example usually in languages:

  • { 1 + 2, 7} reads { (1 + 2), 7} aka {3, 7}
  • or { True && False, True} reads { (True && False), True} aka {False, True}
  • but in roc { a & b: 1, c: 2 } reads { a & (b: 1, c: 2 )} which i did not get in the first few reads

Richard Feldman said:

nice, yeah! Are there any others to add to that list from this thread?

It has not been made explicit but one aspect that goes hand in hand with pattern matching is record and list unpacking/destructuring:

Unpacking/Destructuring Records:

foo = { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3, d: 4 }
{b, ..} = foo # b = 2
{a, b as bar, ..baz} = foo # a = 1, bar = 2, baz = { c: 3, d: 4 }

Unpacking/Destructuring Lists:

foo = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
[a, b, .. ] = foo # a = 1, b = 2
[a, b, .., c ] = foo # a = 1, b = 2, c = 5
[a, b, ..c, d ] = foo # a = 1, b = 2, c = [3, 4], d = 5
of note, the list one has to be in a when because lists might not have any elements in them to destructure :big_smile:
the record one is interesting
the way Rust does it is that you have to say { a, b, .. } = if you want to ignore the other fields
an upside is that if you say exactly { a, b } = and then you add a field to the record later, you'll get a compile error
so it's like an exhaustiveness check

a situation where that's relevant is like a hash, where you really want to incorporate every single field into the hash, and you want an error to remind you to incorporate any new fields that get added later
of course a downside is that you have to write , .. in more places than today :big_smile:
I originally thought we shouldn't do it the way Rust does, but I think it could make more sense in the context of this idea
since .. would appear in more places and feel less out of place
some thoughts on the doc - which looks great btw, thanks so much for putting it together @Romain Lepert! :heart_eyes:

I think this looks way clearer than today:

User a : { firstName : Str, lastName : Str, ..a }

today's syntax, for reference:

User a : { firstName : Str, lastName : Str }a

the original motivation behind that syntax was that I wanted it to be concise for a function to say "I accept these fields and also any others you like" - e.g. today this is the syntax for that:

foo : { a : Str, b : Str }* -> Str

but a thing we've learned is that * is confusing, and I think this is clearer even if it's slightly more verbose:

foo : { a : Str, b : Str, .. } -> Str

I think it might be surprising to people that this use of .. wouldn't be allowed in type aliases or opaque types (you'd have to name the variable, e.g. ..a instead of just ..), but maybe that's fine; the compiler error message could be pretty direct about that

Richard Feldman said:

the way Rust does it is that you have to say { a, b, .. } = if you want to ignore the other fields
an upside is that if you say exactly { a, b } = and then you add a field to the record later, you'll get a compile error

of note, we could make this syntax be opt-in, e.g.

{ a, b, ..{} } =

this could mean "destructure a and b exhaustively, meaning this would be an error if there were any more fields in the record"

regarding this one:

{a, b, ..others} = foo

(where others would be the other fields in the record besides a and b, kinda like [first, ..rest] -> in a list pattern)

the type system doesn't currently support this, so I think we'd want to separately discuss whether we should add support for it. I think the current record type system can handle this, e.g. if I put this into the repl:

\{ a, b, ..others } -> { a, b, others }

...I would expect it to print an inferred type of:

{ a, b, ..others } -> { a, b, others }

so I think the type system change could potentially be as straightforward as adding a new constraint and then solving it, but I'm not sure! It would need further discussion separate from the syntax conversation I think.
is that a useful thing to be able to say? I'm not sure!

user2 = { ..user1, firstName: "Robert", age: 28 }

I think this reads better than today even if we didn't give it any special powers

as in, even if we didn't allow more advanced usages like

{ ..user1, firstName: "Robert", ..user2, age: 28 }

If desired this syntax can only mean record update and disallow record field addition.

I think we'd need to start with that, because the type system doesn't support field addition today, and it would be a big enough change that it should be decoupled from the syntax changes
(as in, we should discuss it separately)

regarding this example:

color : [Green, Gold, ..]
color =
    if x > 0:
        Green # infer type [Green, ..]
    else:
        Gold # infer type [Gold, ..]

I definitely think we should keep the current inference on these as [Green], [Gold], and [Green, Gold]
without the ..
we used to show those as open unions syntactically, and it was a major point of confusion for beginners that just totally disappeared when we switched to the current syntax
and I definitely don't think the problem was the * rather than a ..

that said, I do think that when a tag union argument is open due to when with _ -> in the body of the function, this does look clearer than status quo:

example : [Foo Str, Bar Bool, ..] -> Bool

regarding this:

{a, b as bar, ..baz} = foo

Rust does this as b: bar instead of b as bar, but I like the consistency of b as bar with the rest of the language :thumbs_up:
there are a bunch of places in the language where as should work but hasn't been implemented yet :sweat_smile:

I did notice the doc doesn't mention one of the cases in the first post of this thread, namely combining lists:

[..quickSort lessEq, pivot, ..quickSort greater]
Well, speaking of as, to the record subset concern, maybe we avoid supporting the ..baz and require a { a, b, .. } as baz syntax instead?
Which prevents the ability to get the rest of the record, needing copying of all other fields to a new struct at runtime
I think that could be part of the separate { a, b, ..baz } discussion I think we should split off, yeah

Richard Feldman said:

I did notice the doc doesn't mention one of the cases in the first post of this thread, namely combining lists:

[..quickSort lessEq, pivot, ..quickSort greater]

I don't think we actually need parentheses here, it seems pretty obvious what this means, and we'd just need to parse (..)? + expr as a list literal element for concatenation purposes

to summarize my thoughts:

  • Overall, seems like a great change! Things seem both more consistent and also clearer, and it reduces uses of *, which is positive too.
  • I don't think we should go back to the previous "open tag unions by default" design, even if the syntax has changed from * to ..
  • [..quickSort lessEq, pivot, ..quickSort greater] needs more discussion
  • { a, b, ..others } = needs more investigation (e.g. what would the type system changes need to be?) and more discussion
  • allowing combining records in a way that adds and/or removes fields would require substantial changes to how record types work (and how their code gen works), so that definitely needs a separate discussion
  • Having { a, b, .. } = be required for non-exhaustive record destructures (like Rust) vs. introducing { a, b, ..{} } = vs keeping status quo needs more discussion

I'd like to split off separate threads to discuss these
actually I gotta go to childcare stuff but if anyone else wants to make those threads, I think that's the next step on this!
Done! Unless I missed one
So it seems like this has kind of stalled. Is there more discussion that needs to happen with these threads?
If not, I think we should make some GitHub issues for syntax changes
I can pick that up tomorrow

To summarize my understanding of the 4 "needs more discussion" points above:

  • [..quickSort lessEq, pivot, ..quickSort greater]
    • We are starting with [..(quickSort lessEq), pivot, ..(quickSort greater)] for an unambiguous list spread syntax, and will consider allowing removal of the parentheses later
    • We can also support [first, ..rest] syntax instead of [first, .. as rest] in pattern matching
  • { a, b, ..others } pattern
    • For performance reasons, we are avoiding supporting ..others as "a record without the a and b fields". We will instead move forwards with { a, b, .. } as data, which is only a minor change that leverages our existing as data syntax
  • { a, b, ..other } extending record fields
    • For the same reason as above, we shouldn't support combining field sets, but either { a, b, ..other } or { ..other, a, b } as an update to the other record would work.
    • This can wait, since we now have &foo as a secondary shorthand based on the current { other & a, b } syntax. We should make sure we consciously drop that visual consistency when moving to a new record update syntax
  • { a, b, .. } = as the new required syntax for open record destructures
    • This seems to be (mostly) agreed to improve refactoring as we are less likely to forget to destructure newly added fields
So tomorrow I'll make a parent GitHub issue with some child issues for these points
These changes should be easier to implement than the result/error management changes just proposed, since these .. changes are all mostly for existing syntaxes, it's just new paint
List spread operators don't exist yet, that will be more significant
As well as potentially the { a, b, .. } open record new syntax
Parent issue created: https://github.com/roc-lang/roc/issues/7091
Creating sub issues as well now
6 sub-issues have been created.
I wonder what today's &foo syntax sugar would look like in the .. world :thinking:
if we're not using & for record updates anymore, then using it for &foo would seem confusing and somewhat arbitrary :sweat_smile:
It was an unsolved question when we were discussing these changes.
..foo on its own doesn't seem like it would convey the right thing
And also with the method call changes, without |> I don't think that &foo is a necessary thing either

updatedFoo = &foo data 123

updatedFoo = { foo: 123, ..data }

I saw that @Luke Boswell gave an example of using &foo in his roc-ray? code, but it was him using it multiple times in a chain, which would be worse for performance than a single record update unless LLVM somehow fixed it for us
hm, but I think the original motivating use case was action-state
what would it look like there?

It could always go back to requiring a lambda

\x -> { foo: 123, ..x }

How much is the &foo syntax actually used in practice?

Richard Feldman said:

what would it look like there?

The goal of having &foo simply to facilitate

counter = Elem.translate(Counter.init, .foo, &foo)

Makes me still want to find something less "clunky"
Though admittedly without lenses I still don't know what that is
So record builders were a specific syntax created for combining intermittent state into a conveniently usable format, namely record fields

I think it'd be better for us to generalize the behavior we want to provide to Elem.translate and make a syntax for that, rather than maintain a syntax (&foo) that probably isn't that useful, or maybe even bad for performance

I'll also say that in my action-state experiments it seemed that only passing .foo and &foo wasn't enough to propagate state fully, you also need to pass the state around. Though maybe I was missing something
Yes. You have to pass the state around too
Or wait, isn't that pass around separately when an action occurs?
It could be, but the current API in the latest action-state proposal (v5) doesn't pass the state in the component creation either (a.k.a. button [] [...children])
So someone needs to pass it. I presume Brian Carroll got around it in his design because he uses a poor man's version of lenses to traverse JSON data and use that for updates
Which I think is not viable for most frontend frameworks

AFAICT Richard was trying to avoid passing the state to the component function so that you couldn't capture it into the lambda sets of your action functions (e.g. \event -> Action.update { foo: 123, ..state })

Anyway, basically Svelte and other modern TypeScript frontend frameworks have spoiled me. I think if our ergonomics aren't close to those, then it'll be hard to convince people to move from "Roc's great for backend, but why bother with full stack" to "Roc's slightly less convenient, but it's pretty close and it's really nice to be full stack".

I don't expect we can to the same level as a procedural language because mutation is obviously off the table, but we need to be close.

I'll be trying to think today of a way to extend out record syntax to support some getter and setter creation. Record builders alone still would require the .foo and &foo syntaxes, so I don't think that's ideal.

Something along the lines of:

State : {
    color : Color,
    count : U64,
}

StateAccess : {
    color : {
        get : State -> Color,
        set : State, Color -> State,
    },
    count : {
        get : State -> U64,
        set : State, U64 -> State,
    }
}

(state, stateAccess) =
    { stateful!
        color: Red,
        count: 123,
    }

colorComponent = Elem.translate state stateAccess.color

I know that we're trying to avoid introducing lenses to the language/stdlib, and this gets us closer to making them readily accessible, but we're already pretty close with saying colorLens = (.color, &color).
I haven't really thought about this much, but maybe action-state could benefit from the combination of custom types and methods
So though any tool we add for action-state is available elsewhere, I think that this maintains enough "transparency" to users into what functions we're using to traverse State
That's an interesting thought
like maybe instead of "provide a function to access and update my state" it's like "you need to have methods which do this"
I'm not sure exactly what it would look like, but it seems worth exploring to see if there's something there
Makes sense. I'm trying to consider how we'd use that to bridge the gap between "I have state I manage" and "my parent can manage my state"
I'll think about that today, any thoughts would go in a new topic
To put a bow on the original point of this thread's revival, I think we'd be fine if we kept field and tuple accessors, but left field and tuple updaters out of Roc.
At least field updaters have { foo: 123, ..data } to use

To be fair, there's no equivalent for tuples without listing every single field, but I think we should discourage the usage of tuples larger than even 3 items because at that point, you're probably better off with a record
yeah Elm explicitly dropped support for tuples larger than 3 elements for exactly that reason
but I think vectors and matrices are a compelling reason to support more than 3
